entries.title: About the Course
entries.content: Skills Focus: Practice applying drawing skills with the brush and watercolor techniques. Practice underpainting. Grow in watercolor abilities by continued practice. Draw spheres; study body proportions, foreshortening, curvature, overlapping, texture drawing, and highlighting. Color theory: notan, contrast, monochrome, color studies, and circular value scales.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
If students are in grade 6-8, they should begin with the fast-track lessons and then move on to Level 4. 

Prerequisite: Level 5 or appropriate skill proficiency.

rows.label: Art: Level 6
rows.books: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms and Common Objects||Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Art: Level 6
☐ View Art Teacher Training Tutorials as you need them throughout the years of art study (see Quick Links below). Read Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work by Glass p.1-33 to understand the "why" behind what we do in art lessons, and look over Brushwork: Elementary Brush-Forms for levels 1-3 and Fast Track or Brush Drawing by May Mallam for students in levels 4-8.

☐ Print online images as you need them for lessons. It is very important that students work from printed photos in books or magazines (or printed from the internet) rather than from a device. Computers, iPads, etc. are lightboxes whose illumination affects the way an object is seen. This illumination is not controllable and will lead to confusion and frustration. Students are welcome to view art work or photographs on a device but when they are working from a reference photo it needs to be printed and they should not work from a screen. 

☐ Prepare a large, frameless chalkboard for each student to allow for large shoulder movement. This could be on the backside of their individual drawing boards or one large chalkboard on the wall. Do what works best for your students and classroom. You can view the teacher tutorials to learn how to create a chalkboard for your student(s).

☐ Before you begin the color theory or brush drawing term prepare paint palettes by putting a small amount of each pigment in its own single palette space. Allow to dry for several days. View the video playlist of Art Teacher Training Tutorials to learn how to prepare, clean, and label your palette. 
• Level 6 Palette: Prussian Blue, Hansa Yellow, and Quinacridone Pink

☐ Preview lesson plans to know if you will need a special supply for an upcoming lesson and organize supplies for easy lesson setup. Replenish supplies as needed.

links.label: Foundations (See Section 5: Art Appreciation and Instruction)

blocks.text: Make sure your art supplies are organized and easily accessible. Look ahead to the upcoming lessons and gather supplies needed.
Connect back to the activities and ideas from last lesson.
Teach students the new skill (model it yourself and use the book or video resource in the lesson). Look at examples.
Give students time to practice the new skill. Keep the "Guiding Questions" document (see Appendix) for reference when practicing observational skills.
Have students discuss, observe, and self-evaluate their work.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Vertical, Horizontal and Oblique Brush Strokes 
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes prepared with Quinacridone Pink, Prussian Blue and Hansa Yellow, Art Book 

PREP: Read lesson and preview plate.
"Plate I” Brush Drawing

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use.

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to review basic brush strokes learned in level 4. It is important to do this refresher at the beginning of the year. 

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Beginning Brushstrokes L4 L25, L5 L49, & L6 L1

➜ PRACTICE
Vertical, horizontal, and oblique brush strokes. 
• Practice all directions.
• Change colors as student desires.
"Plate I” Brush Drawing 

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Which brush strokes look best?

➜ NOTE
Students should clean up at the end of each lesson. Clean, dry, and store brush. Leave palette open to dry.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for leaves of different shapes during nature walks. Encourage students to think about how a brush stroke might be used to create a certain leaf in their nature journal.

links.text: Beginning Brushstrokes L4 L25, L5 L49, & L6 L1

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Curved Brush Strokes  
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, Art Book

PREP: Read lesson and preview plate.
"Plate II” Brush Drawing   

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use.

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to review the curved brush strokes learned in level 4. It is important to do this review at the beginning of the year as a refresher.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Curved Brushstrokes L4 L31, L5 L50, & L6 L2

➜ PRACTICE
Curved brush strokes. 
• Practice all lines until students are able to do the stroke with ease.
• Change colors as student desires.
"Plate II” Brush Drawing

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Which brush strokes look best?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Use a different color and make patterns using curved brush strokes or work through the alphabet using cursive letters.

links.text: Curved Brushstrokes L4 L31, L5 L50, & L6 L2

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Memory Drawing: Children at Play  
⍞ Materials: Watercolors, water, brushes, cloth, Art Book, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall figure drawing. You will apply that same knowledge with brush 
  drawing.
• Recall underpainting. You will use this technique to focus on the 
  skeletal motion of the figure.

➜ PRACTICE
Brush drawing
• Remember your observations of children at play.
• Discuss what you remember about the way the body moves. For 
  example, what does it look like when you are running to kick a ball?
• Demonstrate the motion.
• Brush draw children playing, from memory, in your Art Book. 
• Share your drawings with your teacher.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Paint children playing, from memory, in your Art Book.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Brush Drawing People  
⍞ Materials: Watercolors, water, brushes, cloth, Art Book, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall figure drawing. You will apply that same knowledge with brush 
  drawing.
• Recall underpainting. You will use this technique to focus on the 
  skeletal motion of the figure.

➜ NOTE
This video tutorial will take the entire lesson time. 

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
• Watch this video tutorial.
∞ Video Link: Painting Simple Watercolor Figures
• Narrate the video so that you are ready to begin trying this technique 
  in the next lesson.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Brush Drawing People  
⍞ Materials: Watercolors, water, brushes, cloth, Art Book, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall figure drawing. You will apply that same knowledge with brush 
  drawing.
• Recall underpainting. You will use this technique to focus on the 
  skeletal motion of the figure.

➜ NOTE
Students will practice brush drawing people for the next three lessons. It is important to give ample time to practice new skills. The more a student practices, the more they will begin to apply their new skills across the curriculum.  

➜ PRACTICE
• Remember back to the tutorial and how to brush draw the human
  figure. (You can work along with the tutorial if you would feel more 
  confident.)
∞ Video Link: Painting Simple Watercolor Figures
• Fill a few pages with figures in action. Think of men, women and 
  children. What are they doing? What are they wearing?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Talk about the brush strokes that you see in the works studied during picture study.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Brush Drawing People  
⍞ Materials: Watercolors, water, brushes, cloth, Art Book, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall figure drawing. You will apply that same knowledge with brush 
  drawing.
• Recall underpainting. You will use this technique to focus on the 
  skeletal motion of the figure.

➜ NOTE
Students will practice brush drawing people for the next three lessons. It is important to give ample time to practice new skills. The more a students practices, the more they will begin to apply their new skills across the curriculum.  

➜ PRACTICE
• Remember back to the tutorial and how to brush draw the human 
  figure. (You can work along with the tutorial if you would feel more
  confident.)
∞ Video Link: Painting Simple Watercolor Figures
• Fill a few pages with figures in action. Think of men, women and 
  children. What are they doing? What are they wearing?
• Try to incorporate groupings of people doing activities together; for 
  example people sitting at a cafe eating, a baseball game happening.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OCCUPATIONS Illustrate the name of a family member or friend on cardstock to give as a gift.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Talk about all of the different ways that you could apply the watercolor techniques that you have learned in your daily work.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Brush Drawing People  
⍞ Materials: Watercolors, water, brushes, cloth, Art Book, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall figure drawing. You will apply that same knowledge with brush 
  drawing.
• Recall underpainting. You will use this technique to focus on the 
  skeletal motion of the figure.

➜ NOTE
Students will practice brush drawing people for the next three lessons. It is important to give ample time to practice new skills. The more a students practices, the more they will begin to apply their new skills across the curriculum.  

➜ PRACTICE
• Remember the tutorial and how to brush draw the human figure. (You 
  can work along with the tutorial if you would feel more confident.)
∞ Video Link: Painting Simple Watercolor Figures
• Fill a few pages with figures in action. Think of men, women and 
  children. What are they doing? What are they wearing?
• Try to incorporate people of different ages doing different things. Think 
  of a playground with lots of children playing and adults chaperoning.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OCCUPATIONS Write a favorite or original poem on cardstock. Decorate it with a design of ivy. Be creative and take your time. It will take several sessions to do this well. Perhaps use the ivy as a border around the page or as a background. Use waterproof ink. Once you have applied watercolor over pencil you cannot erase without damaging your watercolor. You may want to apply your design and after it dries completely then add your text.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for ivy on your nature walk. You may find it in the stage of going dormant at this time of year. How is it different from the ivy that you have been observing?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Underpainting 
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use. Review underpainting. 

➜ NOTE
In past years you have completed an illustration for history or literature in one lesson. This time, take two lessons to complete the illustration. Plan out your composition.  Begin with an underpainting. Include perspective and people like you have been practicing. 

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Underpainting L4 L29 & L6 L8

➜ PRACTICE
Paint a scene from a recent history or literature lesson. Begin with an underpainting. Try to incorporate people accurately in motion in your scene. You will finish this painting in the next lesson.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell about your painting.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for ivy on your nature walk. You may find it in the stage of going dormant at this time of year. How is it different from the ivy that you have been observing?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Underpainting 
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use. Review underpainting. 

➜ NOTE
In past years you have completed an illustration for history or literature in one lesson. This time, take two lessons to complete the illustration. Plan out your composition. Begin with an underpainting. Include perspective and people like you have been practicing. 

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Underpainting L4 L29 & L6 L8

➜ PRACTICE
Finish painting a scene from a recent history or literature lesson. Begin with an underpainting. Try to incorporate people accurately in motion in your scene.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell about your painting.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Curvature and Foreshortening   
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes
 
➜ RECAP
• Recall monochromatic, gradual washes, changing value with adding or 
  subtracting water, and adding neutral. 
• Recall overlapping.  

➜ NOTE
This lesson on brush drawing leaf curvature and foreshortening will span four lessons. The first two focus on line drawing, the second two will incorporate mass drawing, value and shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Leaf Curvature & Foreshortening L6 L10

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe "Plate 13 - Leaf Curvature” p.53 of Drawing, Design and 
  Craft-Work 
• Study the curvature and foreshortening of the leaves.
• Try drawing the leaves from Plate 13. Draw them just as you see 
  them on the page.
• Focus on the lines for this lesson as opposed to the mass drawing. 
  Your brush should stay up on its tip the entire time.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
What did you do well? What could you improve?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for ivy on your nature walk. You may find it in the stage of going dormant at this time of year. How is it different from the ivy that you have been observing?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Curvature and Foreshortening with House Plant 
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, houseplant
 
➜ RECAP
• Recall curvature & foreshortening.
• Recall overlapping.  

➜ NOTE
This lesson on brush drawing leaf curvature and foreshortening will span four lessons. This is the second to focus on line drawing, the last two will incorporate mass drawing, value and shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Leaf Curvature & Foreshortening L6 L10

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe your houseplant from at least an arm's length away.
• Study the curvature and foreshortening of the leaves. Discuss what you 
  observe.
• Try drawing the leaves from observation. 
• Focus on the lines for this lesson as opposed to the mass drawing. Your 
  brush should stay up on its tip the entire time.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
What did you do well? What could you improve?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OCCUPATIONS Think of other trailing plants that you have observed and create a design with them on cardstock. You could fold your cardstock in half and turn it into a greeting card.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for ivy on your nature walk. You may find it in the stage of going dormant at this time of year. How is it different from the ivy that you have been observing?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 12
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Curvature and Foreshortening with House Plant 
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, houseplant
 
➜ RECAP
• Recall curvature & foreshortening.
• Recall overlapping.  
• Recall mass drawing, value and shadows.

➜ NOTE
This lesson on brush drawing leaf curvature and foreshortening will span four lessons. The first two focused on line drawing, these last two will incorporate mass drawing, value and shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Curvature & Foreshortening en Masse L6 L12

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe your houseplant from at least an arm's length away.
• Study the curvature and foreshortening of the leaves. Discuss what you 
  observe.
• Try drawing the leaves from observation, but this time, do a mass 
   drawing. 
• Focus on applying what you know about value and include the shadows. 
• You will work on this during your next lesson so do not rush but take 
  your time observing and putting all of your effort into applying the skills 
  you have gained. 

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
What did you do well? What could you improve?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OCCUPATIONS Using the first letter of your name, create a beautiful painting. Apply all of the watercolor techniques that you have learned.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe value changes in the paintings you are studying for picture study.

links.text: Curvature & Foreshortening en Masse L6 L12

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Curvature and Foreshortening with House Plant 
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, houseplant
 
➜ RECAP
• Recall curvature & foreshortening.
• Recall overlapping.  
• Recall mass drawing, value and shadows.

➜ NOTE
This is the last of the lessons on brush drawing leaf curvature and foreshortening will span four lessons. The first two focused on line drawing, these last two incorporate mass drawing, value and shadows.

➜ PRACTICE
• Finish your brush drawing of your houseplant
• Study the curvature and foreshortening of the leaves. Discuss what you 
  observe.
• Try drawing the leaves from observation but this time do a mass 
  drawing. 
• Focus on applying what you know about value and include the 
  shadows. 
• Take your time observing and putting all of your effort into applying the 
  skills you have gained. 

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
What did you do well? What could you improve?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Create a design out of acorns on cardstock. You can use them to make a border design or paint them large in the center. Brush draw a favorite quote in the center of your design. This will take several weeks to accomplish.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Spherical Fruits  
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, acorns, spherical fruit (i.e. orange, apple, lemon, etc.)

PREP: Students will need acorns and other spherical fruits this term for observation.                                       

➜ RECAP
• Recall mass drawing with the brush.
• Recall graduated washes and lifting.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Improve Your Watercolor: Do a Value Study

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the tutorial. Listen closely to all she says while working. 
• Discuss observations.

➜ DISCUSS
Were you able to capture the value of the acorn? How well did you apply watercolor techniques? What did you do well? What would you improve next time?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Create a design out of acorns on cardstock. You can use them to make a border design or paint them large in the center. Brush draw a favorite quote in the center of your design. This will take several weeks to accomplish.

links.text: Improve Your Watercolor: Do a Value Study

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 15
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Spherical Fruit  
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, an apple or other spherical fruit, lamp or spot light.

➜ RECAP
• Recall mass drawing with the brush.
• Recall layering.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Figure 3 "Plate VII” Brush Drawing 

➜ PRACTICE
• Set a spherical piece of fruit in front of you. Fix your light source so that
  it is directly over your fruit.
• Discuss observations. How will you paint the highlight with watercolor? 
• Do an observational brush drawing of the fruit.
• Use the layering technique to apply value to the fruit 
  in your brush drawing. Don’t forget the shadows.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your brush drawing to the fruit. How well did you paint the highlight?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Spherical Object
⍞ Materials:Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, spherical object, a lamp or spotlight

➜ RECAP
• Recall mass drawing with the brush.
• Recall layering.
• Recall what your learned in the last video tutorial about applying value 
  to the apple. 

➜ PRACTICE
• Set the spherical object in front of you. Fix your light source so that it is 
  directly over your sphere.
• Discuss observations. How will you paint the highlight with watercolor? 
• Do an observational brush drawing of the object.
• Use the layering technique to apply value to the 
  spherical object in your brush drawing. Don’t forget the shadows.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your brush drawing to the sphere. How well did you apply the various watercolor techniques?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Do a drawing of the same fruit from a different angle in your Art Book. Begin with an underpainting.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: Paint a Bowl of Cherries
⍞ Materials: masking fluid, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Wet on wet, masking, lifting, value, wash, graduated wash.
∞ Video Link: Watercolor Techniques L6 L17 & L8 L49
∞ Video Link: Lifting & Resist (Masking) L6 L17 & L7 L37

➜ PRACTICE
• Work along with the tutorial video.
• Three lesson times are set aside for this lesson. Do not rush.
∞ Video Link: Watercolor Cherries Tutorial
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Discuss how you have applied your knowledge of watercolor techniques and figure drawing to your literature illustration.

links.text: Watercolor Techniques L6 L17 & L8 L49
Lifting & Resist (Masking) L6 L17 & L7 L37

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
Brush Drawing: How to Paint Complex Subjects
⍞ Materials: masking fluid, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Wet on wet, masking, lifting, value, wash, graduated wash.

➜ NOTE
In this video, he uses a light box to trace his subject onto his working paper. We don't do pencil sketches to begin but if you need to, you may. Think about how you have done this in geography for map making. If you have a lightbox, use it, or work on the window with the sunlight coming through. This is to keep you from wasting too much time on the drawing. However, you may free hand draw your subject as well. Or brush sketch with a very watered down color. 

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the tutorial video.
• Decide on a subject to paint based on what you have learned through 
  the video and the skills you have acquired. 
• Begin brush drawing your object. (Optional: pencil sketch your subject 
  or brush sketch with a very watered down color as you have learned 
  in the past.)
• Apply the watercolor techniques you've learned throughout the years. 
• Three lesson times are set aside for this lesson. Do not rush.
∞ Video Link: How to Paint Complex Subjects

➜ DISCUSS 
What techniques did you use to apply value?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 21
lessons.body:

➜ RECAP

➜ PRACTICE.

• Apply the watercolor techniques you've learned throughout the years.

∞ Video Link: How to Paint Complex Subjects

➜ DISCUSS 
What techniques did you use to apply value?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 23
lessons.body:
Exam Week  

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction - Lesson Level 6

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 24
lessons.body:
Exam Week  

➜ EXAMS

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Tertiary Colors and Expanded Color Wheel   
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes, brushes, water, cloth, printed color wheel

PREP: Print color wheel on cardstock.
∞ Link: Color Wheel
∞ Link: Example of an Expanded Color Wheel
∞ Video Link: Create an Expanded Color Wheel L4 L49, L5 L1, & L6 L25

➜ RECAP
Recall all you know about color thus far. Name the colors in the basic color wheel (three primary and three secondary colors). What do you recall about the expanded color wheel and tertiary colors?

➜ INTRO
It is important to review concepts at the beginning of the year. Today, we are going to make a new expanded color wheel to refresh the skills we learned in level 4 and 5. We have two lesson periods to complete this so there is no reason to rush.

➜ DISCUSS
Tertiary colors.
• Tertiary colors are made by mixing a primary color with a secondary 
  color.
• They are red-orange, yellow-orange, yellow-green, blue-green, 
  blue-purple, and red-purple. 
• Always state the primary and then the secondary color when 
  naming the tertiary color.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                

➜ PRACTICE
Expanded color wheel (Complete over two lesson periods; work at your own pace)
• Paint primary colors first; then secondary; then tertiary
• Students will need to mix fresh secondary and tertiary colors. 
• Refer to the example to see placement within the wheel.
• Color placement on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you 
  make a mistake.
• The color wheel will be referred to all term so keep it in a handy 
  place. One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your Art Book. 
∞ Image Link: Example of an Expanded Color Wheel
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Spend time looking outside and naming the colors that you see. Talk about the primary colors that you would use to get those colors.

links.text: Example of an Expanded Color Wheel
Create an Expanded Color Wheel L4 L49, L5 L1, & L6 L25

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Expanded Color Wheel  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, color wheel

➜ RECAP
Recall all you know about color thus far. Name the colors in the basic color wheel (three primary and three secondary colors). What do you recall about the expanded color wheel and tertiary colors?

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to finish our expanded color wheel.

➜ DISCUSS
Tertiary colors.
• Tertiary colors are made by mixing a primary color with a secondary 
  color.
• They are red-orange, yellow-orange, yellow-green, blue-green, 
  blue-purple, and red-purple. 
• Always state the primary and then the secondary color when 
  naming the tertiary color.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                

➜ PRACTICE
Complete expanded color wheel 
• Finish mixing colors and painting them in the appropriate places.
• Refer to the example if you are confused about placement within 
  the wheel.
• Color placement on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you 
  make a mistake.
• The color wheel will be referred to all term so keep it in a handy 
  place. One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your Art Book. 
∞ Image Link: Example of an Expanded Color Wheel
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Make another color wheel with your pastels.

links.text: Example of an Expanded Color Wheel

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Value Scale Chart
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, hole puncher (optional), scissors

➜ RECAP
Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.

➜ PRACTICE
∞ Video Link: Make a Value Scale Chart
• This value chart can be completed in any color, but we are going to use 
  neutral. 
• After you have painted the value scale in neutral, let it dry completely.
• Once the value scale has dried completely, you need to punch (or 
  poke) a clean hole in the center of each value.
• Keep this value scale in a safe place because you will use it in your 
  lessons.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Create an illustration from history.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, red cellophane sheet (optional)

➜ NOTE 
In this tutorial, she uses several different blacks or grays out of the tube. You will only use neutral. However, you should try to mix your own blacks from the colors you have available in your palette. You will do this lesson again in Level 7 Art, adding in new primary colors. 

➜ RECAP
Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch
• Watch the tutorial in full. This will take two lesson times.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Create an illustration from literature. Focus on using a monochromatic color scheme.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, red cellophane sheet (optional)

➜ NOTE 
In this tutorial, she uses several different blacks or grays out of the tube. You will only use neutral. However, you should try to mix your own blacks from the colors you have available in your palette. You will do this lesson again in Level 7 Art, adding in new primary colors. 

➜ RECAP
Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch
• Watch the tutorial in full. This will take two lesson times.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: brush, watercolors, water, cloth, Art Book, simple landscape photo

➜ NOTE 
You will work on this for two lessons. 

➜ RECAP
Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.

➜ PRACTICE
• Recall back to the beginning of the tutorial when she flipped through
  her different versions of the same painting. 
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch
• She referred to her paintings as notan paintings. 
• Notan= (pronounced no-tan) a Japanese term that meaning 'light 
  and dark harmony/balance. ' It is a black & white version of a work 
  of art.
• You need a photo of a simple landscape. Try to find one similar to 
  the one she used, making sure there are no people or animals and 
  only one distant, simple structure, if any. 
• You will spend this lesson time and the next creating several 
  notan paintings of the same simple landscape.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for circular objects on your nature walk. Observe how they change in value.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: brush, watercolors, water, cloth, Art Book, simple landscape photo

➜ NOTE 
You will work on this for two lessons. 

➜ RECAP
• Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall Notan.
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch

➜ PRACTICE
• Create several more simple notan paintings of your landscape photo.
• Try to time yourself and complete one every five minutes. Remember, 
  you are looking for the values and masses- no details.
• Look through all of the versions you have created. How did you do? 
  Which one is your favorite? Why?

➜ DISCUSS 
What techniques did you use to apply value?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Seeing Value in Watercolor
⍞ Materials: brush, watercolors, water, cloth, Art Book, value scale you created, notan paintings you created

➜ RECAP
• Recall value, neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall Notan.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: How to See Values in Watercolor

➜ PRACTICE
• Look back at the notan paintings you created.
• Place your created value scale over one painting at a time to 
  distinguish the values you have created. 

➜ DISCUSS 
What techniques did you use to apply value?

links.text: How to See Values in Watercolor

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Creating a Color Study
⍞ Materials: pastels, cloth, Art Book 

➜ RECAP
• Recall value, neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall Notan.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Creating a Color Study

➜ NOTE
This will take the entire lesson to watch. Listen well to all that he is saying while working. You will hear him state truths you have been learning throughout your art studies.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Creating a Color Study
⍞ Materials: pastels, cloth, Art Book, photo to work from

➜ RECAP
• Recall value, neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall contrasting or complementary colors

➜ PRACTICE
• Decide on a reference photo with contrasting colors. 
• Create a color study of your photo as the artist did in our tutorial. 

➜ DISCUSS 
What techniques did you use to apply value?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Shading with pastels is similar to watercolors. Use pastels to draw an animal from memory in your Art Book.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding or subtracting water. 

➜ INTRO
We are going to do a circular value scale for each of the 12 colors on your color wheel. We will do three colors each lesson and work on this throughout the term. 

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales L6 L35

➜ PRACTICE
Circular (spherical) value scales.
• Draw 12 circles in your Art Book. Each circle should be about three 
  inches in diameter.
• Complete three colors this week.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding or subtracting water. 
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales L6 L35

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to continue to make circular value scales using the 12 colors on the color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
Create circular value scales for three colors in your Art Book.

➜ DISCUSS
How is your shading? Are your circles turning into spheres? Do you need to add more value?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Illustrate a poem using shades of your colors.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 37
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding or subtracting water. 
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales L6 L35

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to continue to make circular value scales using the 12 colors on the color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
Create circular value scales for three colors in your Art Book.

➜ DISCUSS
How is your shading? Are your circles turning into spheres? Do you need to add more value?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 38
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding or subtracting water. 
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales L6 L35

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to finish making circular value scales using the 12 colors on the color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
Create circular value scales for three colors in your Art Book.

➜ DISCUSS
How is your shading? Are your circles turning into spheres? Do you need to add more value?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 39
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Brush Drawing of Animal  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value.
• Recall how to create a shade in watercolor.

➜ PRACTICE
Do a brush drawing of an animal from memory. Apply the knowledge you have gained of shades.

➜ DISCUSS
Share your painting with your teacher. Describe how you applied shading.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Shading with pastels is similar to watercolors. Use pastels to draw an animal from memory in your Art Book. Add a neutral to your colors for the darker values.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 40
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales using Neutral   
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding neutral to your colors. 
• What is a shade?

➜ INTRO
Last term we created circular value scales using water. This term, we will do a similar exercise using neutral to change the color’s value. We will do three colors each lesson and work on this throughout the term. 

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

➜ PRACTICE
Circular value scales using neutral.
• Draw 12 circles in your Art Book. Each circle should be about three 
  inches in diameter.
• Complete three colors this week.

➜ DISCUSS
Talk through the process you went through. What did you learn?

links.text: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 41
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales using Neutral  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding neutral to your colors. 
• What is a shade?
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to continue to make circular value scales using neutral for the 12 colors on the color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
Create circular value scales for three colors in your Art Book using neutral.

➜ DISCUSS
Talk through the process with your teacher.

links.text: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 42
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales using Neutral  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding neutral to your colors. 
• What is a shade?
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to continue to make circular value scales using neutral for the 12 colors on the color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
Create circular value scales for three colors in your Art Book using neutral.

➜ DISCUSS
Talk through the process with your teacher.

links.text: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 43
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Circular Value Scales using Neutral  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding neutral to your colors. 
• What is a shade?
∞ Video Link: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

➜ INTRO
Today we are going to continue to make circular value scales using neutral for the 12 colors on the color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
Create circular value scales for three colors in your Art Book using neutral.

➜ DISCUSS
Talk through the process with your teacher.

links.text: Circular Value Scales Adding Neutral L6 L40

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 44
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Landscape with Underpainting  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, a simple landscape

PREP: Students will need to view a simple landscape for this lesson.

➜ RECAP
• Recall tertiary colors.
• Recall complementary colors.
• Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall how to make swatches in order to match colors.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Simple landscape. Remember to focus on the masses and not the details.

➜ NOTE
It may be more manageable to do a monochromatic painting to focus on value not color.
∞ Image Link: Violet Reflections - Monochromatic Painting

➜ PRACTICE
• Brush draw landscape.
• Prepare color palette and match colors by creating swatches on your 
  paper. 
• Optional, do an underpainting. Use mass drawing and not outline 
  drawing.
• Add value, shading, and shadows.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your brush drawing to the landscape. Consider your colors.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 45
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Flower with Leaves and Stem  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, a flower with leaves and a stem

PREP: Gather a flower with leaves and a stem.

➜ RECAP
• Recall tertiary colors.
• Recall complementary colors.
• Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall how to make swatches in order to match colors.

➜ PRACTICE
Brush drawing
• Set the flower in front of you. 
• Observe the flower and discuss your observations.
• Prepare your color palette and match colors by creating swatches on 
  your paper. This is the most important step as you are studying 
  Color Theory.
• Do a brush drawing of your object. Add value, shading, and shadows. 
  It is okay if you do not get to this during this time. Focus on the 
  accuracy of your colors. 

➜ DISCUSS
What was observed about your colors?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 46
lessons.body:
Color Theory: Spray of Leaves  
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, a spray of leaves on a stem 

➜ RECAP
• Recall tertiary colors.
• Recall complementary colors.
• Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall how to make swatches in order to match colors.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set the spray of leaves in front of you. 
• Observe the piece and discuss your observations.
• Prepare your color palette and match colors by creating swatches on 
  your paper. 
• Do a brush drawing of your object. Add value, shading, and shadows.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
How well did you match colors? Did you focus on value, shading, and shadows?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 47
lessons.body:
Exam Week  

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction - Lesson Level 6

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 48
lessons.body:
Exam Week  

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction - Lesson Level 6

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 49
lessons.body:
Drawing: Figure Drawing  
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board, children at play

PREP: Students will need to observe children playing.

➜ NOTE
For the next few lessons, students will be asked to recall previous lessons and to work from what they've learned as opposed to learning new skills. This allows for time to solidify concepts so that they are able to apply their skills as they move throughout life. Do not skip the assignments of looking in the Glass book. This will help jog student's memory. 

➜ RECAP
Recall figure drawing.

➜ PRACTICE
Figure drawing.
• Observe children at play.
• Discuss what you notice about the way the body moves. For example, 
  what does it look like when you are running to kick a ball?
• Demonstrate the motion.
• Draw children playing in your Art Book. 
• Share your drawings with your teacher.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Observe people all around you. Continue drawing people in action.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 50
lessons.body:
Drawing: Children at Play  
⍞ Materials: Charcoals, Art Book, drawing board, children at play

PREP: Students will need to observe children playing 

➜ RECAP
Recall figure drawing.

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe and draw a child at play.
• Begin with the skeletal lines, making sure that the body position is 
  accurate before putting flesh on the child. You are looking for the lines 
  of action. 
• Once the position is correct, add "flesh" to the bones.
• Think about how clothes wrinkle and fall as arms and legs bend and 
  waists twist. 
• Do not add details to the face or clothing.
• Share your drawings with your teacher.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 51
lessons.body:
Drawing: Landscape Drawing with Overlapping Objects  
⍞ Materials: Charcoal, drawing board, Art Book, erasers

PREP: Students will need to view a landscape for this lesson.

➜ RECAP
• Recall landscapes. What is a horizon line? 
• Recall perspective. What happens to objects that are farther away?
• Recall value. How do you use erasers to help with value when working 
  with charcoal? 
• Remind students that these erasers are drawing tools and not to be 
  used to erase mistakes. 

➜ INTRO
Today, students will do a landscape drawing in their Art Books using charcoal. 

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Drawing Overlapping Objects L6 L51

➜ PRACTICE
Landscape drawing
• Look for a landscape that has simple objects in the foreground that 
  visually overlap objects in the background.
• Complete your landscape focusing on overlapping.

➜ DISCUSS
Share your drawing with your teacher and discuss what you noticed about overlapping.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe the landscape on your nature walk and point out the overlapping objects from your view.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 52
lessons.body:
⬚ 30m Art Instruction: Level 6 - Lesson 52
Drawing: Flower or Leaf Bunch  
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design and Craft-Work, chalk, chalkboard, flower or leaf bunch, charcoal, Art Book

PREP: Gather a flower or bunch of leaves attached together on one twig. 

➜ RECAP
• Recall overlapping.
• Recall mass drawing and how it’s different from outline drawing.
• Recall curvature and foreshortening (we will address this again in lesson 56.)

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Images of leaves and flowers
• Observe the overlapping objects. Notice how the shapes that describe 
  an object change when it is blocked by another object.
“Plate 18 & 19” p.58-59

➜ PRACTICE
• Study a flower or bunch of leaves attached together on one twig. 
• Discuss what you see.
• Draw the specimen on the chalkboard or in your Art Book. Focus on 
  drawing the overlapping parts accurately.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to the bunch of leaves. How well did you draw the overlapping parts?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Do this same exercise with different overlapping objects. Discuss what you see.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 53
lessons.body:
Drawing: Figure Drawing with Proportions  
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, charcoal, Art Book 

➜ RECAP
Recall proportion and figure drawing.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Figure Proportions 

➜ PRACTICE
Do figure drawings on the board or in your Art Book. Focus on body proportions. Don't forget what you learned from observing children at play. 

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
What did you do well? How could you improve your drawing? Are your proportions accurate?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• OCCUPATIONS Using this same knowledge, do brush drawings of figures in your Art Book.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe people when walking by the way. Discuss your observations of bodily proportions.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 54
lessons.body:
Drawing: Figures and Proportion  
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard 

➜ RECAP
• Recall proportion and figure drawing.
• Recall the proper proportions of bodies.

➜ PRACTICE
• Practice drawing people on the board focusing on understanding the 
  correct bodily proportions. 
• Do the figure drawings from what you remember about observing 
  people. The focus is on practicing proportions.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 55
lessons.body:
Drawing: Figures from Memory with Proportion  
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard 

➜ RECAP
Recall proportion of bodies and figure drawing.

➜ PRACTICE
Draw people on the board.
• Draw from memory.
• Focus on body proportions.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Look back in your Art Book at body proportions and compare them with the work you have completed for this lesson.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 56
lessons.body:
Drawing: Handles  
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design and Craft-Work, chalk, chalkboard, objects with handles to observe

➜ RECAP
Recall lines and all of the different types.

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motion.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Handles
• Do you notice how the handles curve and change shape as they seem 
  to come toward you?
"Plate 7: Handles of various types.” p.23

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: What is Foreshortening? 
∞ Video Link: How to Draw the Figure in Perspective-Foreshortening

➜ PRACTICE
Set objects with handles in front of you and practice drawing what you see on the board. 

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Create a page in your Art Book dedicated to handles. Find objects all around you and do observational drawings of the handles.

links.text: How to Draw the Figure in Perspective-Foreshortening

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 57
lessons.body:
Drawing: Handles  
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, objects with handles to observe

PREP: Gather objects with handles; i.e. mugs, pitchers, tea pots, buckets, etc. 

➜ RECAP
• Recall lines and all of the different types.
• Recall foreshortening and curvature.

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motion.

➜ PRACTICE
• Observe the handles on the objects you have collected.
• Discuss observations.
• Notice how the handles curve and change shape as they seem to 
  come toward you.
• Set objects with handles in front of you and practice drawing what you 
  see on the board. 
• Create a page in your Art Book dedicated to handles. Find objects all 
  around you and do observational drawings of the handles.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Observe the foreshortening and curvature of tree branches and limbs. The leaves are likely falling off this time of year and they are easier to see. Do some observational drawings of trees.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 58
lessons.body:
Drawing: Chair 
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl and kneaded erasers, a chair for observing (preferably a simple design)

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motion.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Propping up Drawing Board (Watch until 3:23)

➜ PRACTICE
• Sit on the floor with the chair in front of you. Place the chair 3 feet
  away from you so that you are close to it but can see the top of the 
  seat as well. 
• What do you notice? What shapes make up the chair? Do you see 
  trapezoids, rectangles, triangles, etc.?
• Hold your drawing board in front of you at arm’s length, standing on the 
  floor. Do an observational drawing of the chair.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 59
lessons.body:
Drawing: Chair  
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, charcoal, drawing board, Art Book, chair (such as a living room chair)

➜ RECAP
Recall foreshortening.

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm movements.

➜ PRACTICE
• Sit on the floor 3-4 feet in front of a different chair today. 
• Study the chair.
• Discuss your observations.
• Draw what you see focusing on foreshortening.

➜ DISCUSS
Share your drawing and discuss observations with your teacher.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 60
lessons.body:
Drawing: Human Body and Foreshortening  
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, charcoal, drawing board, Art Book, human model

➜ RECAP
Recall proportions of the human body and foreshortening.

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board using large arm movements.

➜ INTRO
Today, students will do an observational drawing of a person. Ask a friend or family member to be your model.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Foreshortened Poses

➜ PRACTICE
• Ask your model to sit in a way that is foreshortened. 
• Notice the proportions of the human body.
• Draw the figure with charcoal in your Art Book. Apply what you have 
  learned about foreshortening to the human figure.
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Continue doing more drawings of people in your Art Book, whether close-ups of heads or figures in action.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 61
lessons.body:
Drawing: Sphere  
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, one sphere to observe, lamp or spotlight

PREP: Students will need spheres in different sizes this term to observe and manipulate. Spheres include: balls of any kind, Christmas ornaments, oranges, etc. Gather at least five of different sizes. Keep them together so that they are easily accessible for the next couple of weeks.

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall circular value scales.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Shading Light and Form

➜ PRACTICE
• Study the sphere. If it were a shape, what would it be?
• Set the sphere on a table in front of the window.
• Discuss observations.
• Draw the sphere on the board.
• Set the sphere in a place where you can control the light.
• Discuss observations. How is it different or the same as in front of a 
  window?
• Draw this sphere on the board.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Draw and shade spheres in your Art Book with pastels.

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 62
lessons.body:
Drawing: Sphere  
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board, one sphere to observe, lamp or spotlight

➜ RECAP
Recall light source, shading, and shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Shading Light and Form

➜ PRACTICE
• Set the sphere on a table in front of the window.
• Discuss observations. Where is the highlight? Point out the darkest 
  value on the sphere.
• Set the sphere in a place where you can control the light.
• Discuss observations. How is it different or the same as in front of a 
  window? Where is the highlight? Is it smaller or more significant? Point 
  out the darkest value.
• Draw the sphere in your Art Book with pastels. Don’t forget the cast 
  shadows. Are there value changes in the cast shadows?

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 63
lessons.body:
Drawing: Still lIfe in Pastel
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board, spheres to observe; i.e. soccer ball, baseball, volleyball, orange, apple, etc., lamp or spotlight

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: How to Paint a Simple Still Life with Pastels

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the tutorial video completely.
• Pull together some spherical objects that you will begin painting next 
  lesson.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?
lessons.teacherNotes: • OCCUPATIONS Draw and shade spheres in your Art Book with charcoal.

links.text: How to Paint a Simple Still Life with Pastels

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 64
lessons.body:
Drawing: Still lIfe in Pastel
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book (your book is mixed media paper and is suitable for painting with pastels), drawing board, spheres to observe; i.e. soccer ball, baseball, volleyball, orange, apple, etc., lamp or spotlight

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up the spherical objects that you have collected in a pleasing still
  life.
• Set up the light source so that you get some highlights and nice 
  shadows.
• Paint your still life in the style of the tutorial you watched last lesson.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 65
lessons.body:
Drawing: Human Model  
⍞ Materials: Charcoal, erasers, Art Book, drawing board, live model, lamp or spotlight

➜ NOTE
Students will apply the concepts they have learned in creating spherical objects to their portrait drawing.

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.
• Recall proportions of the head and bust.

➜ PRACTICE
• Have your model sit and place the light source behind them, pointing 
  towards their head.
• Study the effect of light on the model's head.
• Discuss observations. Where are the highlights? Point out the darkest 
  values on their head.
• Do an observational drawing of your model, focusing on the head, neck, 
  and shoulders and apply all of your knowledge about shading and 
  highlights.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 66
lessons.body:
Drawing: Human Model  
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board, live model, lamp or spotlight

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.
• Recall proportions of the head and bust.

➜ PRACTICE
• Have your model sit and place the light source behind them, pointing 
  towards their head.
• Study the effect of light on the model's head.
• Discuss observations. Where are the highlights? Point out the darkest 
  values on their head.
• Do an observational drawing of your model, focusing on the head, neck, 
  and shoulders and apply all of your knowledge about shading and 
  highlights.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 67
lessons.body:
Drawing: How to Draw a Cat in Pastel
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: How to Draw a Cat in Pastels

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the tutorial video completely.
• Find an animal photograph that you will begin painting next lesson. You 
  do not need to paint a cat, you can choose any animal.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

links.text: How to Draw a Cat in Pastels

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 68
lessons.body:
Drawing: Informed Animal Painting in Pastel
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book (your book is mixed media paper and is suitable for painting with pastels), drawing board, photo of your animal

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.
• Recall the techniques of adding texture in the tutorial from last lesson. 
  (The Virtual Instructor used pastel pencils to get a fine point. You can
  achieve a fine point by sanding the edge of your pastel down.)

➜ PRACTICE
• Working from your photograph, draw the animal you have chosen.
• Don't forget highlights and shadows
• Paint your animal in the style of the tutorial you watched last lesson.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 69
lessons.body:
Drawing: Cupcake with Pastels
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board, cupcake with frosting (optional)

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Cupcake with Pastels

➜ PRACTICE
• You can either spend this and the next lesson watching the cupcake in 
  pastel tutorial or you can work alongside The Virtual Instructor as he 
  works. 
• Painting desserts is a fun activity to do. Enjoy watching him bring even 
  the smallest speck of sprinkles to life.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 70
lessons.body:
Drawing: Cupcake with Pastels
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board, cupcake with frosting (optional)

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Cupcake with Pastels

➜ PRACTICE
• You can either spend this lesson finishing the cupcake in pastel tutorial 
  or you can work alongside The Virtual Instructor as he works. 
• Painting desserts is a fun activity to do. Enjoy watching him bring even 
  the smallest speck of sprinkles to life.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 71
lessons.body:
Exam Week  

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction - Lesson Level 6

lessons.title: 30m Art: Level 6 - Lesson 72
lessons.body:
Exam Week  

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction - Lesson Level 6

terms.content:
· Paint a scene with simple watercolor figures engaged in an activity.

· Paint a spray of leaves in outline, including curvature and foreshortening.

· Illustrate a poem from the term and explain the watercolor and brush drawing techniques you included.

· Explain these terms from color theory: monochromatic, color swatch.

· Complete a circular value scale using your favorite color.

· Explain what neutral does when added to other pigments.

· Paint a sphere with pastels, perfectly demonstrating shading, shadows and highlights.

· Draw and label the proper proportions of an adult human.

· Explain what foreshortening is and how to apply it in your artwork.
